topic: Two friends both apply for the same job
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the office hummed, a grating soundtrack to my mounting fury. I stared at the printed confirmation email, the words 'Congratulations, you have been selected' mocking me from the screen. Liam. Of course. He got the position I’d been angling for, the one I’d spent six months perfecting my portfolio for. I slammed my laptop shut, the sound echoing sharply in the quiet corner of the shared workspace. My jaw clenched so tight I thought my teeth might crack. I walked over to the water cooler, gripping the cold metal until my knuckles were white. "This is ridiculous," I muttered, the sound rough in my throat. I felt a hot, prickly sensation rising up my neck, a desperate need to rip the paper in half. I needed to find something, anything, to channel this blinding frustration before I completely lost control.

[story 2]

"Seriously? Again?" I hissed, the words barely audible above the clatter of keyboards. I watched Liam casually accept the offer, his smile easy and infuriatingly relaxed. My hands were shaking slightly as I reread the job description, the lines blurring into meaningless ink. A deep, internal churning started in my stomach, a heavy, unpleasant pressure that made breathing shallow. I pushed my chair back so violently it scraped across the linoleum floor. "He just… he just gets everything handed to him," I spat out, my voice tight with barely suppressed agitation. I paced three steps toward the window, my fists bunching at my sides. The sheer unfairness of it made my vision swim for a second. I wanted to scream at the universe, to shake the very air, but I kept my mouth shut, focusing instead on the rigid set of my shoulders, trying to contain the seismic shift happening inside me.

[story 3]

I couldn't look at him. Every glance felt like an assault. The air in the small breakroom suddenly felt thick, suffocating. I gripped my coffee mug so hard the ceramic threatened to splinter. My breath hitched, coming in short, sharp gasps. "This is a joke," I managed, the tone laced with a brittle edge that I immediately regretted. I turned away, walking toward the exit, my gait stiff and jerky. Each step was a deliberate act of control, a desperate attempt to keep the internal storm from spilling over into a physical outburst. I could feel the heat radiating off my skin, a flush creeping up my neck, fueled by this overwhelming sense of injustice. I needed space. I needed silence. I needed to burn this whole situation down.
